content:
# 2 Thessalonians Chapter 3 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2TH_003_page_001.jpg

---

**1** Finally, brothers, pray for us, that the word of the Lord may run and be glorified, just as it was also among you,

**2** and that we may be delivered from perverse and wicked people, for not all have the faith.

**3** But faithful is the Lord, who will strengthen you and guard you from the evil one.

**4** And we have confidence in the Lord concerning you, that what we command, you both do and will continue to do.

**5** And may the Lord direct your hearts into the love of God and into the steadfastness of Christ.

**6** Now we command you, brothers, in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, to withdraw yourselves from every brother who walks in a disorderly manner and not according to the tradition which they received from us.

**7** For you yourselves know how it is necessary to imitate us, because we were not disorderly among you,

**8** nor did we eat bread from anyone without paying, but in toil and hardship, working night and day, so as not to be a burden to any of you --

**9** not because we do not have the right, but in order to give ourselves as an example to you, so that you would imitate us.

**10** For even when we were with you, we commanded you this: that if anyone is not willing to work, neither let him eat.

**11** For we hear that some among you are walking in a disorderly manner, not working at all but being busybodies.

**12** Now such persons we command and exhort in the Lord Jesus Christ, that working with quietness they eat their own bread.

**13** But you, brothers, do not grow weary in doing good.

**14** And if anyone does not obey our word through this letter, take note of that person and do not associate with him, so that he may be put to shame.

**15** And do not regard him as an enemy, but admonish him as a brother.

**16** Now may the Lord of peace himself give you peace at all times and in every way. The Lord be with you all.

**17** The greeting is in my own hand -- Paul -- which is a sign in every letter; this is how I write.

**18** The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all.

---

## Council Notes

**On the Greek manuscript (Sinaiticus):** The source image shows 2 Thessalonians 3 spanning columns 2 and 3 of the folio, with verse markers at the bottom indicating 2 Ths. 2:17, 2 Ths. 3:8, 2 Ths. 3:18, and the beginning of Hebrews 1:1. The text is written in the continuous uncial script characteristic of Sinaiticus, with the subscription ΠΡΟΣ ΘΕΣΣΑΛΟΝΙΚΕΙΣ Β visible before the transition to Hebrews.

**Verse 2 -- "perverse and wicked":** The Greek ΑΤΟΠΩΝ ΚΑΙ ΠΟΝΗΡΩΝ presents two distinct qualities. ΑΤΟΠΟΣ ("out of place") was rendered "perverse" (EMET, LOGOS), "warped" (PNEUMA). The consensus uses "perverse" as the more established English equivalent while pairing it with "wicked" (PNEUMA) rather than "evil" (EMET, LOGOS) to differentiate ΠΟΝΗΡΩΝ here from the ΤΟΥ ΠΟΝΗΡΟΥ ("the evil one") in verse 3.

**Verse 3 -- "the evil one":** All three translators render ΤΟΥ ΠΟΝΗΡΟΥ as personal ("the evil one") rather than abstract ("evil"), and the consensus follows. The article and the personal contrast with the Lord's faithfulness favor a personal referent.

**Verse 5 -- "steadfastness" vs. "endurance":** The Greek ΥΠΟΜΟΝΗ carries both active endurance and patient steadfastness. EMET chose "endurance," while LOGOS and PNEUMA chose "steadfastness." The consensus adopts "steadfastness" as slightly better suited to the contemplative tone of the prayer and the genitive "of Christ," which holds both subjective (Christ's own steadfastness) and objective (steadfastness directed toward Christ) meaning.

**Verse 6 -- "they received" (Sinaiticus variant):** All three translators correctly noted that Sinaiticus reads ΠΑΡΕΛΑΒΟΣΑΝ (third person, "they received") rather than the Byzantine ΠΑΡΕΛΑΒΕΤΕ ("you received"). PNEUMA, however, rendered it "you received" in its translation text despite noting the variant. The consensus preserves the Sinaiticus reading "they received." PNEUMA also rendered "brother" as "believer" -- the consensus retains "brother" as the literal rendering of ΑΔΕΛΦΟΥ.

**Verse 11 -- Greek wordplay:** All three translators recognized the paronomasia ΜΗΔΕΝ ΕΡΓΑΖΟΜΕΝΟΥΣ ΑΛΛΑ ΠΕΡΙΕΡΓΑΖΟΜΕΝΟΥΣ. The consensus preserves "not working at all but being busybodies," which captures the rhetorical contrast as closely as English permits.

**Verse 17 -- Autograph authentication:** Paul's practice of taking the pen from his amanuensis to sign the closing personally is well documented (1 Cor 16:21, Gal 6:11, Col 4:18). The ΣΗΜΕΙΟΝ ("sign") gains particular urgency in light of the concern about forged letters in 2 Thessalonians 2:2.

**Verse 18 -- "with you all":** Sinaiticus includes ΠΑΝΤΩΝ ("all"), emphasizing that grace extends to the entire congregation, including those who have been admonished for disorderly conduct. The inclusive benediction tempers the disciplinary severity of verses 6-15.

**Gender-inclusive language:** PNEUMA consistently rendered ΑΔΕΛΦΟΙ as "brothers and sisters." While this reflects modern English convention and the likely mixed audience, the consensus retains "brothers" as a formal-equivalent rendering of the Greek, consistent with the translation's overall approach. A footnote-level acknowledgment that ΑΔΕΛΦΟΙ encompassed the full congregation is implicit throughout.

-- SOPHIA, Arbiter of the Council
